lemmas.root_meaning: to be, to exist, to become
to twist, to argue, to dispute
to do, to work, to make

etymology.pattern_meaning: simple past third person masculine singular action
etymology.derivation: ق.و.ل (root: to say) + فَعَلَ pattern = قَالَ (he said)

etymology.root_meaning: to be, to exist, to become
etymology.pattern_meaning: simple past third person masculine singular of copular/existential verb
etymology.derivation: ك.و.ن (root: to be, to exist) + فَعَلَ pattern = كَانَ (he was)
etymology.cultural_note: Primary existential/copular verb in Arabic, fundamental to sentence structure

etymology.derivation: ق.د.م (root: to advance) + فَعَّلَ pattern (doubled second radical) = قَدَّمَ (he presented, submitted)

etymology.root_meaning: to twist, to argue, to dispute
etymology.derivation: ج.د.ل (root: to argue) + فَاعَلَ pattern = جَادَلَ (he argued, disputed)
خ.ب.ر (root: to know) + أَفْعَلَ pattern (causative prefix) = أَخْبَرَ (he informed)
ع.ر.ف (root: to know) + اِفْتَعَلَ pattern (reflexive) = اِعْتَرَفَ (he confessed, acknowledged)

etymology.root_meaning: to do, to work, to make
etymology.pattern_meaning: causative request — to request or cause someone to do something
etymology.derivation: ع.م.ل (root: to work) + اسْتَفْعَلَ pattern (causative prefix) = اسْتَعْمَلَ (he used, employed)

etymology.pattern_meaning: craft, profession, or practice noun derived from an action root
etymology.derivation: ج.ر.ح (root: to wound) + فِعَالَة pattern = جِرَاحَة (surgery — the practice of medical wounding/cutting)
etymology.semantic_field: medicine, surgery, wound treatment, surgical practice
etymology.cultural_note: From the same root as جَرْح (wound), the noun جِرَاحَة elevated the action to a healing profession

etymology.pattern_meaning: pertaining to or characteristic of the noun root
etymology.derivation: م.د.ن (root: city, civilization, via مَدِينَة) + relational adjective = مَدَنِي (civil, pertaining to civilization)
etymology.semantic_field: civilization, city, civil affairs, urban society
etymology.cultural_note: Derives from مَدِينَة (madina = city), linking urban civilization to civic life

etymology.derivation: From Greek κινηματογράφος (kinematographos, from kinema 'motion' + grapho 'to write'), borrowed via European languages (English cinema, French cinéma)
etymology.cultural_note: Borrowed in the early 20th century with the introduction of film technology to the Arab world

{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":33883,"duration_api_ms":33800,"ttft_ms":17118,"num_turns":2,"result":"**Etymology generation complete.** I've created structured etymology data for all 10 words following Arabic morphological principles:

**Key insights applied:**

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
• **Native roots → patterns**: Words 1–9 all derive from consonantal roots combined with standard morphological patterns (fa'ala, fa''ala, if'tala, etc.). Each pattern carries semantic weight: fa''ala adds intensity/causation; if'tala marks reflexive action; istif'ala marks requesting/causing.
• **Root-meaning discipline**: The derivations trace how each root's core meaning (ع.م.ل = "to work") transforms via pattern into the target meaning (استعمل = "to use"). This respects the critical rule: NEVER invent a foreign origin for native roots.
• **Loanword handling**: Only سينما (cinema) is borrowed, and only because the meaning itself is a borrowed modern concept from film technology. The derivation traces the Greek → European → Arabic path.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

All 10 words now have complete etymology objects with semantic fields and cultural context where relevant. The data supports language learners in understanding word origins at the root and pattern say, to speak","pattern":"fa'ala","pattern_meaning":"simple past third person masculine singular action","derivation":"ق.و.ل (root: to say) + فَعَلَ pattern = قَالَ (he said)","semantic_field":"speech, communication, utterance, expression","related_loanwords":[]}},{"lemma_id":385,"etymology":{"root_meaning":"to be, to exist, to become","pattern":"fa'ala","pattern_meaning":"simple past third person masculine singular of copular/existential verb","derivation":"ك.و.ن (root: to be, to exist) + فَعَلَ pattern = كَانَ (he was)","semantic_field":"existence, being, state, becoming","related_loanwords":[],"cultural_note":"Primary existential/copular verb in Arabic, fundamental to sentence structure"}},{"lemma_id":3081,"etymology":{"root_meaning":"to advance, to precede","pattern":"fa''ala","pattern_meaning":"intensified or causative action verb","derivation":"ق.د.م (root: to advance) + فَعَّلَ pattern (doubled second radical) = قَدَّمَ (he presented, submitted)","semantic_field":"advancement, presentation, precedence, submission","related_loanwords":[]}},{"lemma_id":2942,"etymology":{"root_meaning":"to twist, to argue, to dispute","pattern":"fa'ala with medial alif","pattern_meaning":"reciprocal or iterative action","derivation":"ج.د.ل (root: to argue) + فَاعَلَ pattern = جَادَلَ (he argued, disputed)","semantic_field":"argument, dispute, debate, contention","related_loanwords":[]}},{"lemma_id":3082,"etymology":{"root_meaning":"to know, to be informed","pattern":"af'ala","pattern_meaning":"causative action — to cause knowledge","derivation":"خ.ب.ر (root: to know) + أَفْعَلَ pattern (causative prefix) =

etymology.derivation: qāla = basic action of speaking = to say
etymology.cultural_note: A very common Form I hollow verb whose middle root consonant wāw appears as long ā in the past stem.

etymology.derivation: kāna = basic state of existing/being = to be
etymology.cultural_note: A central Form I hollow verb used both for existence and as an auxiliary in tense and aspect constructions.

etymology.derivation: qaddama = cause to go forward = present, submit, put forward
etymology.cultural_note: The doubled middle consonant in Form II gives the sense of making something advance or placing it before someone.

etymology.derivation: jādala = engage another in disputing = to argue
etymology.cultural_note: The root's older sense of twisting is metaphorically extended to verbal contest, as in turning arguments back and forth.

etymology.derivation: akhbara = cause someone to know news/information = to inform, tell
etymology.cultural_note: Related to khabar, 'news' or 'report', a key term in Arabic grammar and transmission literature.

etymology.derivation: i'tarafa = come to recognize/admit something oneself = to confess
etymology.cultural_note: The Form VIII pattern often marks an inward or self-involving action, fitting the sense of acknowledging a fact.

etymology.pattern_meaning: seeking, causing, or treating as an action
etymology.derivation: ista'mala = put something to work = to use
etymology.cultural_note: From the same root as 'amal, 'work' or 'deed'; the Form X verb developed the practical sense of employing something for a purpose.

etymology.pattern_meaning: practice, profession, or act related to the root
etymology.derivation: jirāḥa = the practice of treating wounds by cutting = surgery
etymology.cultural_note: The meaning developed naturally from wounds and cutting to the medical art of operative treatment.

etymology.derivation: madanī = related to the city/civic order = civil
etymology.cultural_note: Contrasts with military or rural contexts; the same root is connected with madīna, 'city'.

etymology.derivation: From French 'cinéma', shortened from 'cinématographe' (motion-picture apparatus), ultimately from Greek roots meaning movement and writing/recording.
etymology.cultural_note: Entered modern Arabic with European film technology and culture in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.
